setting.note: The Nottinghamshire coalfield that made Lawrence. He renamed his home village of Eastwood "Bestwood" and the pit "Minton", but placed them exactly on the ground he grew up on: the collieries where his father worked, the Chambers family's Haggs Farm that became Miriam's, and Nottingham itself, where Paul takes the train to work and to the art school. It brings the English mining Midlands into the atlas.

rights.originalWork: Public domain. D. H. Lawrence, first published 1913; the author died in 1930.
rights.territoryNotes: Public domain in the United States (published 1913) and in the United Kingdom and the EU, where the author died more than 70 years ago (1930).
rights.attribution: D. H. Lawrence, Sons and Lovers (1913). Text from Project Gutenberg.

palette.note: The collier's red mouth in a grey, coal-dirtied face, straight from the miners tramping home from the pit. Clears WCAG AA on paper.
